content.content:
1	# AGENTS.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **Work repo** (`/` — project root): the project's generated
8	   artifact — code, prose, image, song, whatever it produces.
9	2. **Bot repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
10	
11	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
12	
13	**Terminology.** "Work repo" and "bot repo" are the standard
14	names; write them as two words, adding a hyphen only when the
15	pair sits directly in front of another noun ("work-repo
16	commit", "bot-repo side"). Notes:
17	
18	- `.claude` is the bot repo's *path*, not its name — commands
19	  (`-R .claude`) and ochid paths (`/.claude/<chid>`) keep the
20	  literal path.
21	- The vc-x1 CLI's scope name for the work repo is `code`
22	  (`--scope=code|bot|code,bot`).
23	- "Work commit" / "Work-N" (capitalized) is a cycle-stage term
24	  (see [Cycle Protocol](#cycle-protocol)), not a repo name; a
25	  generic commit landing in the work repo is a "work-repo
26	  commit", never a bare "work commit".
27	
28	**Committing vs pushing.** Default to `vc-x1 push` — it commits and
29	publishes both repos together, each carrying one `ochid:` pointing at
30	the other (see [Cycle Protocol](#cycle-protocol) and
31	[ochid trailers](#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)). Use a bare
32	`jj commit` (see [jj Basics](#jj-basics)) only when:
33	
34	- the change will never be published — no `ochid:`;
35	- the commit will be squashed away before its series is pushed
36	  (loop-and-squash) — no `ochid:`; it disappears in the squash;
37	- the commit will be pushed later as a non-top commit — `jj commit`
38	  and add its `ochid:` now, because `vc-x1 push` stamps only the
39	  topmost commit it pushes, never the ancestors.
40	
41	**What "commit" and "push" mean.** In an instruction, "commit",
42	"push", and "commit + push" all mean `vc-x1 push` — land *and*
43	publish — unless stated otherwise. A bare `jj commit` is asked for
44	by name: "local commit", "save locally", "just `jj commit`" (the
45	cases above). So the spoken default is publish; the local-only
46	save is the named exception. The approval around a push —
47	interactive by default, waived only by an explicit scoped
48	delegation — is the cycle protocol's
49	[Pushing policy](notes/cycle-protocol.md#policy).
50	
51	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
52	
53	- Work repo: `.` (project root)
54	- Bot repo: `.claude`
55	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
56	
57	## Working Directory
58	
59	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
60	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
61	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
62	command failures downstream.
63	
64	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
65	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
66	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
67	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
68	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
69	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
70	not a stylistic choice.
71	
72	Run **one command per shell invocation** — don't bundle several
73	steps into a single compound script (`a && b; c`). Bundling hides
74	which step produced which output and makes a failure ambiguous;
75	executing one at a time keeps the details visible and reviewable.
76	Exceptions are a genuine pipeline (`grep | sort`) or a tight,
77	inseparable pair where the join is the point.
78	
79	## File reads — read the slice you need
80	
81	Long notes files are appended to over time. Read only the
82	slice your task needs; grep or read further on demand.
83	
84	- **`notes/todo.md`** (the routine acquaint read) — first
85	  ~60 lines covers intro + `## In Progress` + the top of the
86	  ranked `## Todo` (priorities, #1 highest). `Read` with
87	  `offset=0, limit=60`. `## Ideas` sits below `## Todo`; read
88	  further only when chasing a lower-ranked entry, an Idea, a
89	  `[N]` ref, or auditing the whole list.
90	- **`notes/todo-backlog.md`** — the long-tail backlog
91	  (lower-priority entries below the ranked `## Todo`). Read
92	  only when picking up a backlog item; grep to locate it
93	  first.
94	- **`notes/bugs.md`** — the bug list. Small; read whole
95	  when triaging a bug or chasing the `## Bugs` pointer in
96	  todo.md.
97	- **`notes/done.md`** + **`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`** —
98	  historical / append-mostly. Scan headings first
99	  (`grep '^## ' notes/chores/chores-NN.md`), then read only
100	  the section you need.
101	
102	**Why:** the routine read should stay small. `notes/todo.md`
103	grows every cycle, so the backlog and bugs live in sibling
104	files rather than inline; the same "slice you need" rule
105	applies to historical files.
106	
107	## Memory
108	
109	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
110	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
111	AGENTS.md it provides no capability AGENTS.md doesn't already cover,
112	and it loses on discoverability:
113	
114	- **AGENTS.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
115	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
116	  (human or bot).
117	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
118	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
119	
120	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
121	durable context in AGENTS.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
122	
123	## Plain synopsis after technical explanations
124	
125	When a conversational reply centers on a technical explanation
126	(measurement theory, statistics, hardware behavior), end it
127	with a short plain-language synopsis — no jargon, no symbols —
128	so the reader can check their understanding against the
129	technical version.
130	
131	**Why:** the technical form is precise but easy to misread;
132	the plain form catches misunderstandings early, when they are
133	cheap.
134	
135	**How to apply:** conversation only, not notes files (a notes
136	entry should already lead with the why). Mark it clearly (e.g.
137	"The plain version:"). A reply that is already plain needs no
138	synopsis.
139	
140	## Speculation marker
141	
142	Durable text the bot writes — AGENTS.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
143	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
144	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
145	enters the text, prefix it with "We think ..." (a royal "we") so a
146	reader can tell the measured from the inferred.
147	
148	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
149	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
150	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
151	in the written record.
152	
153	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
154	marker. Prefix with "We think ..." (or a close variant like
155	"Our guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
156	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
157	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
158	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
159	
160	## jj Basics
161	
162	**Use jj, not git, for version-control operations** (status, log,
163	diff, commit, push, history rewrite). jj coexists with the git
164	backend, so the repo *can* be driven with raw `git`, but this
165	project's workflow — bookmarks, the working-copy `@` model, ochid
166	trailers — is expressed in jj terms; reaching for `git` invites
167	state that doesn't match the jj documentation here. There is no
168	`jj mv`: to move/rename a tracked file, just `mv` it on disk and
169	jj detects the rename by content.
170	
171	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
172	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
173	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
174	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
175	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
176	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
177	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
178	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
179	- The bot repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
180	  session because session data updates continuously.
181	- `jj rebase` uses `--onto`/`-o` to name the destination(s).
182	
183	## Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)
184	
185	The cross-reference between the work repo and the bot repo is
186	what makes the dual-repo work: every commit points at its
187	counterpart in the other repo, so the "what" (code) and the
188	"why / how" (session) stay linked across time. That pointer is the
189	**ochid** (Other Change ID) git trailer.
190	
191	A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier that
192	survives rebases and `describe`s (unlike the commit ID / git SHA,
193	which changes on rewrite). An **ochid** trailer carries the
194	counterpart commit's chid as a workspace-root-relative path:
195	
196	- Paths start with `/` — the workspace root, i.e. the work repo
197	  (the project root). `/.claude` is the bot sub-repo.
198	- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **work repo**.
199	- `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the **bot
200	  repo**.
201	
202	Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the end of
203	the commit body, using the chid's **12-character** prefix:
204	
205	```
206	ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqowktp   # points to a bot-repo change
207	ochid: /wtpmottvxqzl           # points to a work-repo change
208	```
209	
210	How many, and which direction:
211	
212	- **Work-repo commits** each carry one
213	  `ochid: /.claude/<bot-chid>` — the bot repo's change ID.
214	- **The bot-repo commit** carries one `ochid: /<work-chid>` per
215	  work-repo commit in that push. More than one occurs on a Merge
216	  non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in the cycle).
217	
218	Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs (first
219	line work repo, second bot repo).
220	
221	### Resolvability
222	
223	A change ID travels with its commit: a **pushed** commit resolves
224	to the same chid in every clone — cloning the bot repo gave
225	the published `main` tip the same chid as an existing clone. We
226	think jj carries the change ID in the git commit object, so it
227	survives `jj git clone` / fetch.
228	
229	The local-only case is the **working-copy `@`**: jj mints a fresh
230	random chid for `@` in each clone, so an unpushed `@` is never a
231	stable ochid target. This is why a bot-repo ochid names `@-`
232	(the last committed change), not `@`.
233	
234	### .vc-config.toml
235	
236	Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
237	location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
238	without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:
239	
240	```toml
241	# In the work repo (workspace root):
242	[workspace]
243	path = "/"
244	
245	# In .claude (sub-repo):
246	[workspace]
247	path = "/.claude"
248	```
249	
250	## Prose form
251	
252	Long-lived prose on this project follows one basic shape: a short intro
253	that explains the *why* or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet
254	list for the details. Wrap lines at ≤72 cols (bullet continuations
255	indent two spaces). Avoid wall-of-prose paragraphs — they hide the
256	structure that bullets make scannable.
257	
258	Surfaces that use this shape:
259	
260	- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files —
261	  see [Doc comments](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method).
262	- Commit message bodies (both work-repo and bot-repo). The
263	  ≤50-col title is the commit-specific add-on; see
264	  [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow).
265	- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` — see
266	  [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
267	- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` when an entry needs more
268	  than one line of detail. Pure one-liners are still fine.
269	
270	Bullet *content* differs by surface:
271	
272	- **Commit bodies** — bullets are file-by-file: one bullet per file
273	  changed, file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
274	  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Source of truth for the
275	  mechanical edit list.
276	- **Chores / todo / done** — bullets are conceptual (design points,
277	  structural notes, the "what landed and why" at a notch above
278	  file-list granularity). Never a copy of the commit's edit list —
279	  see [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
280	- **Doc comments** — bullets are whatever structure fits (fields,
281	  cases, invariants).
282	
283	### Problem + plan shape
284	
285	`## In Progress` cycle blocks, chores section intros, and `## Todo`
286	entries use a sharper form of the same shape:
287	
288	- **Problem statement** (the why) — one or two sentences;
289	  don't pad with intent, don't restate the plan.
290	- **Plan bullets** (the what/when) — formality differs by
291	  surface:
292	  - In Progress / chores: a committed ladder, one step per
293	    commit — see
294	    [Conventional-commit shape](#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit)
295	    for the per-step title + `(current)` / `(done)` form.
296	  - Todo entries: rough informal bullets, no numbering;
297	    formalized only when the entry is picked up into a
298	    cycle.
299	
300	### Semicolons inside bullets
301	
302	A bullet that joins multiple clauses with semicolons (`A; B; C`)
303	is a list hiding inside running prose — break the clauses into
304	sub-bullets so the structure shows. Semicolons in running prose
305	(intro paragraphs, sentence-joins) are fine. Not absolute: very
306	short clauses or tight pairs can stay joined inside a bullet when
307	breaking would be more noise than signal.
308	
309	### Conventional-commit shape (ladder / chores / commit)
310	
311	A ladder step, its chores section, and its commit description
312	share a *title* shape — a
313	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/)
314	title over [Prose form](#prose-form) detail. They differ in the
315	title's prefix / marker (below) and in bullet *content* — commit
316	bodies are file-by-file, ladder / chores conceptual (see "Bullet
317	*content* differs by surface" above). The shared template:
318	
319	```
320	<optional - X.Y.Z[-N]> <title>   # <title> is the commit's `<type>: <desc>`
321	<optional prose intro>
322	  - <optional item>
323	    <optional prose intro>
324	      - <optional sub-item>
325	      ...
326	```
327	
328	The three surfaces apply it as:
329	
330	- **Ladder step** (`notes/todo.md` `## In Progress`): the title is
331	  prefixed with the version — `X.Y.Z-N <title>` — and
332	  carries a `(current)` / `(done)` marker. The bare three-element
333	  `X.Y.Z` (no `-N`) is the close-out step. Detail is bulleted,
334	  never `;`-joined inline.
335	- **Chores section** (`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`): no version
336	  prefix — the `##` header *is* the bare title, with a
337	  `Commits: [[ref]]` line first under it (empty until backfilled —
338	  see [Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references)).
339	- **Commit description**: no version prefix — the title is the
340	  ≤50-col first line; the body is the prose (file-by-file for the
341	  work repo, per [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow)).
342	
343	The title is **identical** across all three for a given step, so a
344	step's ladder entry, its chores `##` header, and its commit title
345	line up verbatim — pick the commit title first and reuse it.
346	
347	That identity is **per step**, not per cycle: each step in a
348	cycle gets its own distinct descriptive title — never one shared
349	cycle title uniquified by a step marker. The cycle's chores
350	section header carries the anticipated *close-out* title. To keep
351	a cycle's commits collectable with one `git log --grep`, give the
352	step titles a common greppable stem (e.g. `config loader`).
353	
354	## Notes file conventions
355	
356	Conventions the bot follows when writing notes files
357	(`notes/todo.md`, `notes/todo-backlog.md`, `notes/bugs.md`,
358	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`, `notes/done.md`). One source
359	of truth lives here; [`notes/README.md`](notes/README.md)
360	points back.
361	
362	### Notes references
363	
364	Reference *citations* are double-bracketed so the brackets render
365	— `[[N]]`, or `[[2]],[[3]]` for several (comma-separated, not
366	`[2,3]` or `[[2]][[3]]`). The `[N]:` definitions in a file's
367	`# References` section and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
368	links stay single-bracketed.
369	
370	### Reference numbering
371	
372	Every note file (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`,
373	`chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a file-local `# References`
374	section at the bottom. Reference numbers are scoped to that
375	file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in `chores-01.md`
376	are independent slots that may point at completely different
377	URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.
378	
379	Treat `[N]` like a **footnote**: the number is a local slot, only
380	meaningful within its file's `# References`. So a `[N]` *citation*
381	(bare `[N]`, or doubled `[[N]]`) never reuses another file's number; to cite a target a sibling
382	file references, pick your own next-local slot and define it
383	(the same target may carry a different number in each file). A
384	`[N]` *inside a code span* (`` `[72]` ``) is different — that's
385	a quoted identifier: literal text naming a ref-key (often from
386	another file's namespace), data, not a citation; it needs no
387	definition here. To point at a section of another file from
388	prose, use an inline link with an anchor —
389	`[that section](../chores-07.md#…)` — not a bare number.
390	
391	A `chores-NN.md` `# References` entry is usually a
392	`/notes/<file>.md#anchor` (or `/ARCHITECTURE.md`) path, but may
393	also be a **commit reference** —
394	`[N]: <commit-url-with-12-hex-SHA> "<full-40-hex-SHA>"`, cited by
395	a section's `Commits:` line. See
396	[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references) for the
397	why and the exact shape.
398	
399	A file's `# References` can be **re-packed** to a contiguous
400	`[1]..[N]` in first-citation-appearance order — walk the file's
401	prose in document order (`todo.md`: `## Todo` then `## Done`;
402	`chores-NN.md`: top to bottom) and number refs as their first
403	`[[N]]` citation appears. This is a file-local rewrite — only
404	that file's `[[N]]` citations and `[N]:` definitions move; every
405	target and sibling file is untouched. A `[[N]]` inside a `` ` ``
406	code span is a literal token, not a citation, and is left alone.
407	Do it opportunistically (when the namespace has drifted enough to
408	annoy), not on a schedule: `todo.md` fragments fastest (entries
409	land and get pruned every cycle) and is the usual candidate;
410	`chores-NN.md` / `done.md` are append-mostly and only need it
411	after an unusual event (e.g. a bulk retrofit that allocated slots
412	out of document order).
413	
414	### Markdown anchor links
415	
416	GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
417	characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
418	**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
419	on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
420	`:`). General markdown reference:
421	[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
422	publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
423	de-facto reference implementation is
424	[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).
425	
426	### Todo format
427	
428	`todo.md` is organized into `## In Progress`, `## Todo`
429	(strict priority rank, #1 highest; long-tail backlog in
430	[todo-backlog.md](notes/todo-backlog.md)), `## Ideas`,
431	`## Bugs` (pointer to [bugs.md](notes/bugs.md)), and
432	`## Done` sections. Each item is a short description with
433	reference links to more detail.
434	
435	`## Todo` and `## Bugs` entries carry explicit `1.` `2.` …
436	numbers in the source. For `## Todo` the number is its
437	**priority rank** (#1 highest, descending); for `## Bugs`
438	it's just an index. They're for grepping and at-a-glance
439	"let's do #1" — **not stable IDs**: reorder (to
440	reprioritize), insert, or delete freely, then
441	`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` renumbers and normalizes
442	continuation-line indent, so any given number is positional.
443	**To refer to a Todo durably, name it by its title — a
444	plain, greppable text mention.** Not its number (positional,
445	renumbered) and not a markdown link: a numbered list item has
446	no anchor to link to.
447	
448	Numbering helps a human orient in a long list but makes links
449	difficult and fragile — especially an external reference
450	pointing in, which can't be auto-fixed when the list
451	renumbers. A robust fix (a number-free anchor, or a
452	number-tolerant dereference that matches the title slug and
453	wildcards the numeric prefix — a GitHub slug like `5-foo` is
454	encoded, not opaque, so the title is recoverable) is a
455	`validate-numbering` design question, out of scope here.
456	
457	`vc-x1 fix-todo` alone only previews; `vc-x1 validate-todo`
458	is the read-only check. The `## Done` section keeps `-`
459	bullets — items aren't referenced by number once completed.
460	
461	Example shape:
462	
463	```
464	# Todo
465	1. Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
466	2. Fix bug Y [[1]]
467	
468	# Done
469	- Fixed issue Z [[2]],[[3]]
470	
471	[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
472	[2]: issues.md#issue-z
473	[3]: fixes.md#fix-z
474	```
475	
476	### Retiring Done entries
477	
478	`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently
479	shipped work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md`
480	at two natural beats:
481	
482	- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix)
483	  commit ships, decide which prior entries are no longer
484	  needed for nearby context and migrate them.
485	- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep
486	  before bumping the version-of-record.
487	
488	Migration mechanics:
489	
490	- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
491	  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
492	- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
493	  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are
494	  file-local, so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is
495	  fine).
496	- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer
497	  cited by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` /
498	  `## Done`. This frees the numbers for future reuse.
499	
500	## Chores conventions
501	
502	### Headings and entries that record a commit
503	
504	A commit's title is reused verbatim across its records — see
505	[Conventional-commit shape](#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit)
506	for the rule. Beyond the chores `##` header, that same string is
507	used for the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry and any `[N]`
508	reference to that section. Titles carry **no `(<version>)`
509	suffix** — see
510	[Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
511	for why.
512	E.g. the chores header `## refactor: extract config loader`
513	and the Done line `- refactor: extract config loader [[3]]`.
514	The `## Done` entry uses the close-out commit's title.
515	
516	This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
517	`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
518	inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
519	Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
520	default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
521	line a record up with its commit.
522	
523	A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
524	progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
525	`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
526	commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
527	for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
528	dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
529	(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).
530	
531	Any pre-existing sections and `## Done` entries that predate
532	this convention keep their free-form text; the convention
533	applies going forward.
534	
535	### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record
536	
537	A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
538	header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
539	bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
540	subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
541	structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
542	commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
543	changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
544	scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
545	for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
546	other.
547	
548	When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
549	hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
550	own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
551	wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
552	change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
553	with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
554	normal ref direction).
555	
556	**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
557	to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
558	drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
559	narrative; `Commits:` links them.
560	
561	### Chores commit references
562	
563	The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
564	line citing the git commit(s) that section records:
565	
566	```
567	## refactor: extract config loader
568	
569	Commits: [[3]]
570	
571	<intro paragraph...>
572	```
573	
574	`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
575	[Reference numbering](#reference-numbering)),
576	**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
577	or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
578	literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
579	`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
580	GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
581	puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
582	40-hex SHA** in the title slot:
583	
584	```
585	[3]: https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
586	```
587	
588	- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
589	  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
590	  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
591	- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
592	  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
593	  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
594	  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.
595	
596	**Timing.** A commit's SHA isn't stable until it lands on a
597	**permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived release/patch
598	branch) — a rebase or squash rewrites it on the way. A commit
599	can't record its own SHA, so the fill lands one push later:
600	every section opens with an **empty `Commits:`**, and each push
601	backfills the `Commits:` of the commits the previous push made
602	permanent. On a topic branch a section waits until the branch
603	lands. The commit itself is the record, and
604	`git log --grep "<title>"` finds it. See cycle-protocol
605	[Commits backfill](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill).
606	
607	## Cycle Protocol
608	
609	Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation (`X.Y.Z-0`) →
610	Work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) → Close-out (bare
611	`X.Y.Z`). Each commit runs through a per-commit flow whose
612	validation step is medium-specific — the Rust example is the
613	cargo cycle (fmt → clippy → test → install); skip-able for
614	notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. The full protocol
615	— numbering, per-commit flow, reviewing changes, close-out,
616	pushing, ochid trailers, sub-cycles — lives in
617	[`notes/cycle-protocol.md`](notes/cycle-protocol.md). Read
618	it before any commit work — and before any push, cycle or
619	not: pushes always need per-push user approval (see
620	[Pushing policy](notes/cycle-protocol.md#policy)).
621	
622	**Versioning.** How this repo numbers cycles, where the
623	version-of-record lives, and its bump cadence are
624	project-specific and documented in
625	[versioning.md](notes/versioning.md) — the single source of
626	truth that AGENTS.md and cycle-protocol.md refer to
627	abstractly.
628	
629	**Hard stop after push/finalize.** Once a push or bot-repo
630	finalize is invoked (`vc-x1 push` does both), do no further
631	work — no verification, no summary, no next-step offers —
632	until the user speaks. Put all closing words *before* the
633	invoke. The harness rejects an empty turn, so it may force a
634	visible token after the tool returns; if so, emit a bare
635	acknowledgment only (e.g. "landed") — never a summary or more
636	work. Post-push verification happens next turn at the user's
637	direction. See
638	[After push or finalize](notes/cycle-protocol.md#after-push-or-finalize-stop-and-wait).
639	
640	**`vc-x1 push` behaviors to keep in mind.** Two, independent
641	of project language:
642	
643	- **Preflight** runs the cargo cycle (fmt → clippy → test). On
644	  a cargo project it works as-is, so `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`
645	  runs it before the review gate. On a project with no cargo
646	  cycle — or any time preflight can't run — invoke `vc-x1 push
647	  <bookmark> --from message ...` to skip preflight and the
648	  interactive review gate, doing the diff review in
649	  conversation first.
650	- **ochid trailers** are injected by `vc-x1 push` itself —
651	  don't hand-write them into the commit body or
652	  `--title`/`--body`.
653	
654	
655	## Code Conventions
656	
657	### Doc comments on every file, function, and method
658	
659	Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
660	function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
661	one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
662	comment exists, not that it be long. Doc comments follow the
663	[Prose form](#prose-form) shape (intro + bullets).
664	
665	This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
666	default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
667	module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
668	inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
669	non-obvious WHY.
670	
671	**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
672	`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
673	into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
674	any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
675	its own line in the rendered help.
676	
677	### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)
678	
679	The default in non-test code is to **not** use `.unwrap()`,
680	`.expect(…)`, or the `.unwrap_or*(…)` siblings — prefer a shape
681	that doesn't need them (`match`, `if let`, slice patterns,
682	infallible-by-construction APIs), which is usually also the
683	clearer code. This is a lean, not a ban: some sites are
684	legitimately best expressed with one. Two kinds of risk, both
685	in scope:
686	
687	- `.unwrap()` / `.expect(…)` — a panic path.
688	- `.unwrap_or(…)` / `.unwrap_or_default()` /
689	  `.unwrap_or_else(…)` — no panic, but a silently substituted
690	  value that can hide a wrong result.
691	
692	When one is used, three obligations attach:
693	
694	- A trailing `// OK: …` comment justifying why the call is
695	  acceptable:
696	  - `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real
697	    precondition, invariant, or domain reason. Preferred
698	    whenever the reason isn't self-evident.
699	  - `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context
700	    (e.g. `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc →
701	    empty title).
702	  - Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
703	    Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require
704	    a decoder ring for readers seeing the code out of context.
705	- **Alert the user in conversation** when introducing one, so
706	  the site gets reviewed and appropriate uses are learned —
707	  don't let it ride in silently on a larger diff.
708	- For `.unwrap()` / `.expect(…)`, an `#[allow(...)]` at the
709	  site, because Rust projects enable the project-wide lints in
710	  `Cargo.toml`:
711	
712	  ```toml
713	  [lints.clippy]
714	  unwrap_used = "warn"
715	  expect_used = "warn"
716	  ```
717	
718	  Every panicking site is then opt-in and visible in the diff;
719	  clippy (run by preflight) catches any that slip through. The
720	  `_or*` siblings have no clippy lint — they are covered by the
721	  comment convention and the conversational alert. (The
722	  vc-template-x1 template's `CargoRust.toml` seeds a base
723	  `Cargo.toml` with this section already in place.)
724	
725	```rust
726	let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
727	let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious
728	
729	match matches.len() {
730	    1 => {
731	        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
732	        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
733	        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
734	    }
735	    // ...
736	}
737	```
738	
739	Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
740	correct test behavior.
741	
742	### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input
743	
744	When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
745	ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
746	result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
747	redoing misaligned work costs much more.
748	
749	### Recognize when stuck
750	
751	If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
752	without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
753	requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
754	Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
755	on X."
756

file.content:
# AGENTS.md - Bot Instructions

## Project Structure

This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:

1. **Work repo** (`/` — project root): the project's generated
   artifact — code, prose, image, song, whatever it produces.
2. **Bot repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.

Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.

**Terminology.** "Work repo" and "bot repo" are the standard
names; write them as two words, adding a hyphen only when the
pair sits directly in front of another noun ("work-repo
commit", "bot-repo side"). Notes:

- `.claude` is the bot repo's *path*, not its name — commands
  (`-R .claude`) and ochid paths (`/.claude/<chid>`) keep the
  literal path.
- The vc-x1 CLI's scope name for the work repo is `code`
  (`--scope=code|bot|code,bot`).
- "Work commit" / "Work-N" (capitalized) is a cycle-stage term
  (see [Cycle Protocol](#cycle-protocol)), not a repo name; a
  generic commit landing in the work repo is a "work-repo
  commit", never a bare "work commit".

**Committing vs pushing.** Default to `vc-x1 push` — it commits and
publishes both repos together, each carrying one `ochid:` pointing at
the other (see [Cycle Protocol](#cycle-protocol) and
[ochid trailers](#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)). Use a bare
`jj commit` (see [jj Basics](#jj-basics)) only when:

- the change will never be published — no `ochid:`;
- the commit will be squashed away before its series is pushed
  (loop-and-squash) — no `ochid:`; it disappears in the squash;
- the commit will be pushed later as a non-top commit — `jj commit`
  and add its `ochid:` now, because `vc-x1 push` stamps only the
  topmost commit it pushes, never the ancestors.

**What "commit" and "push" mean.** In an instruction, "commit",
"push", and "commit + push" all mean `vc-x1 push` — land *and*
publish — unless stated otherwise. A bare `jj commit` is asked for
by name: "local commit", "save locally", "just `jj commit`" (the
cases above). So the spoken default is publish; the local-only
save is the named exception. The approval around a push —
interactive by default, waived only by an explicit scoped
delegation — is the cycle protocol's
[Pushing policy](notes/cycle-protocol.md#policy).

## Repo Paths (relative from project root)

- Work repo: `.` (project root)
- Bot repo: `.claude`
  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)

## Working Directory

Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
command failures downstream.

When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
not a stylistic choice.

Run **one command per shell invocation** — don't bundle several
steps into a single compound script (`a && b; c`). Bundling hides
which step produced which output and makes a failure ambiguous;
executing one at a time keeps the details visible and reviewable.
Exceptions are a genuine pipeline (`grep | sort`) or a tight,
inseparable pair where the join is the point.

## File reads — read the slice you need

Long notes files are appended to over time. Read only the
slice your task needs; grep or read further on demand.

- **`notes/todo.md`** (the routine acquaint read) — first
  ~60 lines covers intro + `## In Progress` + the top of the
  ranked `## Todo` (priorities, #1 highest). `Read` with
  `offset=0, limit=60`. `## Ideas` sits below `## Todo`; read
  further only when chasing a lower-ranked entry, an Idea, a
  `[N]` ref, or auditing the whole list.
- **`notes/todo-backlog.md`** — the long-tail backlog
  (lower-priority entries below the ranked `## Todo`). Read
  only when picking up a backlog item; grep to locate it
  first.
- **`notes/bugs.md`** — the bug list. Small; read whole
  when triaging a bug or chasing the `## Bugs` pointer in
  todo.md.
- **`notes/done.md`** + **`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`** —
  historical / append-mostly. Scan headings first
  (`grep '^## ' notes/chores/chores-NN.md`), then read only
  the section you need.

**Why:** the routine read should stay small. `notes/todo.md`
grows every cycle, so the backlog and bugs live in sibling
files rather than inline; the same "slice you need" rule
applies to historical files.

## Memory

Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
AGENTS.md it provides no capability AGENTS.md doesn't already cover,
and it loses on discoverability:

- **AGENTS.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
  (human or bot).
- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.

Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
durable context in AGENTS.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.

## Plain synopsis after technical explanations

When a conversational reply centers on a technical explanation
(measurement theory, statistics, hardware behavior), end it
with a short plain-language synopsis — no jargon, no symbols —
so the reader can check their understanding against the
technical version.

**Why:** the technical form is precise but easy to misread;
the plain form catches misunderstandings early, when they are
cheap.

**How to apply:** conversation only, not notes files (a notes
entry should already lead with the why). Mark it clearly (e.g.
"The plain version:"). A reply that is already plain needs no
synopsis.

## Speculation marker

Durable text the bot writes — AGENTS.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
enters the text, prefix it with "We think ..." (a royal "we") so a
reader can tell the measured from the inferred.

**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
in the written record.

**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
marker. Prefix with "We think ..." (or a close variant like
"Our guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.

## jj Basics

**Use jj, not git, for version-control operations** (status, log,
diff, commit, push, history rewrite). jj coexists with the git
backend, so the repo *can* be driven with raw `git`, but this
project's workflow — bookmarks, the working-copy `@` model, ochid
trailers — is expressed in jj terms; reaching for `git` invites
state that doesn't match the jj documentation here. There is no
`jj mv`: to move/rename a tracked file, just `mv` it on disk and
jj detects the rename by content.

- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
- The bot repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
  session because session data updates continuously.
- `jj rebase` uses `--onto`/`-o` to name the destination(s).

## Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)

The cross-reference between the work repo and the bot repo is
what makes the dual-repo work: every commit points at its
counterpart in the other repo, so the "what" (code) and the
"why / how" (session) stay linked across time. That pointer is the
**ochid** (Other Change ID) git trailer.

A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier that
survives rebases and `describe`s (unlike the commit ID / git SHA,
which changes on rewrite). An **ochid** trailer carries the
counterpart commit's chid as a workspace-root-relative path:

- Paths start with `/` — the workspace root, i.e. the work repo
  (the project root). `/.claude` is the bot sub-repo.
- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **work repo**.
- `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the **bot
  repo**.

Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the end of
the commit body, using the chid's **12-character** prefix:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqowktp   # points to a bot-repo change
ochid: /wtpmottvxqzl           # points to a work-repo change
```

How many, and which direction:

- **Work-repo commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<bot-chid>` — the bot repo's change ID.
- **The bot-repo commit** carries one `ochid: /<work-chid>` per
  work-repo commit in that push. More than one occurs on a Merge
  non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in the cycle).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs (first
line work repo, second bot repo).

### Resolvability

A change ID travels with its commit: a **pushed** commit resolves
to the same chid in every clone — cloning the bot repo gave
the published `main` tip the same chid as an existing clone. We
think jj carries the change ID in the git commit object, so it
survives `jj git clone` / fetch.

The local-only case is the **working-copy `@`**: jj mints a fresh
random chid for `@` in each clone, so an unpushed `@` is never a
stable ochid target. This is why a bot-repo ochid names `@-`
(the last committed change), not `@`.

### .vc-config.toml

Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:

```toml
# In the work repo (workspace root):
[workspace]
path = "/"

# In .claude (sub-repo):
[workspace]
path = "/.claude"
```

## Prose form

Long-lived prose on this project follows one basic shape: a short intro
that explains the *why* or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet
list for the details. Wrap lines at ≤72 cols (bullet continuations
indent two spaces). Avoid wall-of-prose paragraphs — they hide the
structure that bullets make scannable.

Surfaces that use this shape:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files —
  see [Doc comments](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method).
- Commit message bodies (both work-repo and bot-repo). The
  ≤50-col title is the commit-specific add-on; see
  [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow).
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` — see
  [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` when an entry needs more
  than one line of detail. Pure one-liners are still fine.

Bullet *content* differs by surface:

- **Commit bodies** — bullets are file-by-file: one bullet per file
  changed, file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Source of truth for the
  mechanical edit list.
- **Chores / todo / done** — bullets are conceptual (design points,
  structural notes, the "what landed and why" at a notch above
  file-list granularity). Never a copy of the commit's edit list —
  see [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
- **Doc comments** — bullets are whatever structure fits (fields,
  cases, invariants).

### Problem + plan shape

`## In Progress` cycle blocks, chores section intros, and `## Todo`
entries use a sharper form of the same shape:

- **Problem statement** (the why) — one or two sentences;
  don't pad with intent, don't restate the plan.
- **Plan bullets** (the what/when) — formality differs by
  surface:
  - In Progress / chores: a committed ladder, one step per
    commit — see
    [Conventional-commit shape](#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit)
    for the per-step title + `(current)` / `(done)` form.
  - Todo entries: rough informal bullets, no numbering;
    formalized only when the entry is picked up into a
    cycle.

### Semicolons inside bullets

A bullet that joins multiple clauses with semicolons (`A; B; C`)
is a list hiding inside running prose — break the clauses into
sub-bullets so the structure shows. Semicolons in running prose
(intro paragraphs, sentence-joins) are fine. Not absolute: very
short clauses or tight pairs can stay joined inside a bullet when
breaking would be more noise than signal.

### Conventional-commit shape (ladder / chores / commit)

A ladder step, its chores section, and its commit description
share a *title* shape — a
[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/)
title over [Prose form](#prose-form) detail. They differ in the
title's prefix / marker (below) and in bullet *content* — commit
bodies are file-by-file, ladder / chores conceptual (see "Bullet
*content* differs by surface" above). The shared template:

```
<optional - X.Y.Z[-N]> <title>   # <title> is the commit's `<type>: <desc>`
<optional prose intro>
  - <optional item>
    <optional prose intro>
      - <optional sub-item>
      ...
```

The three surfaces apply it as:

- **Ladder step** (`notes/todo.md` `## In Progress`): the title is
  prefixed with the version — `X.Y.Z-N <title>` — and
  carries a `(current)` / `(done)` marker. The bare three-element
  `X.Y.Z` (no `-N`) is the close-out step. Detail is bulleted,
  never `;`-joined inline.
- **Chores section** (`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`): no version
  prefix — the `##` header *is* the bare title, with a
  `Commits: [[ref]]` line first under it (empty until backfilled —
  see [Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references)).
- **Commit description**: no version prefix — the title is the
  ≤50-col first line; the body is the prose (file-by-file for the
  work repo, per [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow)).

The title is **identical** across all three for a given step, so a
step's ladder entry, its chores `##` header, and its commit title
line up verbatim — pick the commit title first and reuse it.

That identity is **per step**, not per cycle: each step in a
cycle gets its own distinct descriptive title — never one shared
cycle title uniquified by a step marker. The cycle's chores
section header carries the anticipated *close-out* title. To keep
a cycle's commits collectable with one `git log --grep`, give the
step titles a common greppable stem (e.g. `config loader`).

## Notes file conventions

Conventions the bot follows when writing notes files
(`notes/todo.md`, `notes/todo-backlog.md`, `notes/bugs.md`,
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`, `notes/done.md`). One source
of truth lives here; [`notes/README.md`](notes/README.md)
points back.

### Notes references

Reference *citations* are double-bracketed so the brackets render
— `[[N]]`, or `[[2]],[[3]]` for several (comma-separated, not
`[2,3]` or `[[2]][[3]]`). The `[N]:` definitions in a file's
`# References` section and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
links stay single-bracketed.

### Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`,
`chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a file-local `# References`
section at the bottom. Reference numbers are scoped to that
file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in `chores-01.md`
are independent slots that may point at completely different
URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

Treat `[N]` like a **footnote**: the number is a local slot, only
meaningful within its file's `# References`. So a `[N]` *citation*
(bare `[N]`, or doubled `[[N]]`) never reuses another file's number; to cite a target a sibling
file references, pick your own next-local slot and define it
(the same target may carry a different number in each file). A
`[N]` *inside a code span* (`` `[72]` ``) is different — that's
a quoted identifier: literal text naming a ref-key (often from
another file's namespace), data, not a citation; it needs no
definition here. To point at a section of another file from
prose, use an inline link with an anchor —
`[that section](../chores-07.md#…)` — not a bare number.

A `chores-NN.md` `# References` entry is usually a
`/notes/<file>.md#anchor` (or `/ARCHITECTURE.md`) path, but may
also be a **commit reference** —
`[N]: <commit-url-with-12-hex-SHA> "<full-40-hex-SHA>"`, cited by
a section's `Commits:` line. See
[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references) for the
why and the exact shape.

A file's `# References` can be **re-packed** to a contiguous
`[1]..[N]` in first-citation-appearance order — walk the file's
prose in document order (`todo.md`: `## Todo` then `## Done`;
`chores-NN.md`: top to bottom) and number refs as their first
`[[N]]` citation appears. This is a file-local rewrite — only
that file's `[[N]]` citations and `[N]:` definitions move; every
target and sibling file is untouched. A `[[N]]` inside a `` ` ``
code span is a literal token, not a citation, and is left alone.
Do it opportunistically (when the namespace has drifted enough to
annoy), not on a schedule: `todo.md` fragments fastest (entries
land and get pruned every cycle) and is the usual candidate;
`chores-NN.md` / `done.md` are append-mostly and only need it
after an unusual event (e.g. a bulk retrofit that allocated slots
out of document order).

### Markdown anchor links

GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
`:`). General markdown reference:
[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
de-facto reference implementation is
[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).

### Todo format

`todo.md` is organized into `## In Progress`, `## Todo`
(strict priority rank, #1 highest; long-tail backlog in
[todo-backlog.md](notes/todo-backlog.md)), `## Ideas`,
`## Bugs` (pointer to [bugs.md](notes/bugs.md)), and
`## Done` sections. Each item is a short description with
reference links to more detail.

`## Todo` and `## Bugs` entries carry explicit `1.` `2.` …
numbers in the source. For `## Todo` the number is its
**priority rank** (#1 highest, descending); for `## Bugs`
it's just an index. They're for grepping and at-a-glance
"let's do #1" — **not stable IDs**: reorder (to
reprioritize), insert, or delete freely, then
`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` renumbers and normalizes
continuation-line indent, so any given number is positional.
**To refer to a Todo durably, name it by its title — a
plain, greppable text mention.** Not its number (positional,
renumbered) and not a markdown link: a numbered list item has
no anchor to link to.

Numbering helps a human orient in a long list but makes links
difficult and fragile — especially an external reference
pointing in, which can't be auto-fixed when the list
renumbers. A robust fix (a number-free anchor, or a
number-tolerant dereference that matches the title slug and
wildcards the numeric prefix — a GitHub slug like `5-foo` is
encoded, not opaque, so the title is recoverable) is a
`validate-numbering` design question, out of scope here.

`vc-x1 fix-todo` alone only previews; `vc-x1 validate-todo`
is the read-only check. The `## Done` section keeps `-`
bullets — items aren't referenced by number once completed.

Example shape:

```
# Todo
1. Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
2. Fix bug Y [[1]]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [[2]],[[3]]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z
```

### Retiring Done entries

`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently
shipped work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md`
at two natural beats:

- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix)
  commit ships, decide which prior entries are no longer
  needed for nearby context and migrate them.
- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep
  before bumping the version-of-record.

Migration mechanics:

- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are
  file-local, so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is
  fine).
- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer
  cited by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` /
  `## Done`. This frees the numbers for future reuse.

## Chores conventions

### Headings and entries that record a commit

A commit's title is reused verbatim across its records — see
[Conventional-commit shape](#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit)
for the rule. Beyond the chores `##` header, that same string is
used for the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry and any `[N]`
reference to that section. Titles carry **no `(<version>)`
suffix** — see
[Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
for why.
E.g. the chores header `## refactor: extract config loader`
and the Done line `- refactor: extract config loader [[3]]`.
The `## Done` entry uses the close-out commit's title.

This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
line a record up with its commit.

A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).

Any pre-existing sections and `## Done` entries that predate
this convention keep their free-form text; the convention
applies going forward.

### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record

A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
other.

When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
normal ref direction).

**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
narrative; `Commits:` links them.

### Chores commit references

The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
line citing the git commit(s) that section records:

```
## refactor: extract config loader

Commits: [[3]]

<intro paragraph...>
```

`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
[Reference numbering](#reference-numbering)),
**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
40-hex SHA** in the title slot:

```
[3]: https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
```

- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.

**Timing.** A commit's SHA isn't stable until it lands on a
**permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived release/patch
branch) — a rebase or squash rewrites it on the way. A commit
can't record its own SHA, so the fill lands one push later:
every section opens with an **empty `Commits:`**, and each push
backfills the `Commits:` of the commits the previous push made
permanent. On a topic branch a section waits until the branch
lands. The commit itself is the record, and
`git log --grep "<title>"` finds it. See cycle-protocol
[Commits backfill](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill).

## Cycle Protocol

Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation (`X.Y.Z-0`) →
Work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) → Close-out (bare
`X.Y.Z`). Each commit runs through a per-commit flow whose
validation step is medium-specific — the Rust example is the
cargo cycle (fmt → clippy → test → install); skip-able for
notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. The full protocol
— numbering, per-commit flow, reviewing changes, close-out,
pushing, ochid trailers, sub-cycles — lives in
[`notes/cycle-protocol.md`](notes/cycle-protocol.md). Read
it before any commit work — and before any push, cycle or
not: pushes always need per-push user approval (see
[Pushing policy](notes/cycle-protocol.md#policy)).

**Versioning.** How this repo numbers cycles, where the
version-of-record lives, and its bump cadence are
project-specific and documented in
[versioning.md](notes/versioning.md) — the single source of
truth that AGENTS.md and cycle-protocol.md refer to
abstractly.

**Hard stop after push/finalize.** Once a push or bot-repo
finalize is invoked (`vc-x1 push` does both), do no further
work — no verification, no summary, no next-step offers —
until the user speaks. Put all closing words *before* the
invoke. The harness rejects an empty turn, so it may force a
visible token after the tool returns; if so, emit a bare
acknowledgment only (e.g. "landed") — never a summary or more
work. Post-push verification happens next turn at the user's
direction. See
[After push or finalize](notes/cycle-protocol.md#after-push-or-finalize-stop-and-wait).

**`vc-x1 push` behaviors to keep in mind.** Two, independent
of project language:

- **Preflight** runs the cargo cycle (fmt → clippy → test). On
  a cargo project it works as-is, so `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`
  runs it before the review gate. On a project with no cargo
  cycle — or any time preflight can't run — invoke `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --from message ...` to skip preflight and the
  interactive review gate, doing the diff review in
  conversation first.
- **ochid trailers** are injected by `vc-x1 push` itself —
  don't hand-write them into the commit body or
  `--title`/`--body`.


## Code Conventions

### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long. Doc comments follow the
[Prose form](#prose-form) shape (intro + bullets).

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
its own line in the rendered help.

### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)

The default in non-test code is to **not** use `.unwrap()`,
`.expect(…)`, or the `.unwrap_or*(…)` siblings — prefer a shape
that doesn't need them (`match`, `if let`, slice patterns,
infallible-by-construction APIs), which is usually also the
clearer code. This is a lean, not a ban: some sites are
legitimately best expressed with one. Two kinds of risk, both
in scope:

- `.unwrap()` / `.expect(…)` — a panic path.
- `.unwrap_or(…)` / `.unwrap_or_default()` /
  `.unwrap_or_else(…)` — no panic, but a silently substituted
  value that can hide a wrong result.

When one is used, three obligations attach:

- A trailing `// OK: …` comment justifying why the call is
  acceptable:
  - `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real
    precondition, invariant, or domain reason. Preferred
    whenever the reason isn't self-evident.
  - `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context
    (e.g. `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc →
    empty title).
  - Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
    Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require
    a decoder ring for readers seeing the code out of context.
- **Alert the user in conversation** when introducing one, so
  the site gets reviewed and appropriate uses are learned —
  don't let it ride in silently on a larger diff.
- For `.unwrap()` / `.expect(…)`, an `#[allow(...)]` at the
  site, because Rust projects enable the project-wide lints in
  `Cargo.toml`:

  ```toml
  [lints.clippy]
  unwrap_used = "warn"
  expect_used = "warn"
  ```

  Every panicking site is then opt-in and visible in the diff;
  clippy (run by preflight) catches any that slip through. The
  `_or*` siblings have no clippy lint — they are covered by the
  comment convention and the conversational alert. (The
  vc-template-x1 template's `CargoRust.toml` seeds a base
  `Cargo.toml` with this section already in place.)

```rust
let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious

match matches.len() {
    1 => {
        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
    }
    // ...
}
```

Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
correct test behavior.

### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input

When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
redoing misaligned work costs much more.

### Recognize when stuck

If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
on X."

< The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
---
> A chores section's `Commits:` line cites the commit(s) it
> records, by SHA — but a SHA isn't stable until the commit
> lands on a **permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived
> release/patch branch that won't be rewritten); a rebase or
> squash rewrites it on the way. So:
> 
> - A chores section is **opened with an empty `Commits:`** line.
> - **Backfill once the commit is on a permanent branch**,
>   where its SHA is final. A commit can't record its own SHA
>   (that would change the hash), so the fill always lands one
>   push later: **each push backfills the `Commits:` of the
>   commits the previous push made permanent.** On a topic
>   branch the sections instead wait until the branch lands —
>   so no SHA is ever written that a later rebase could
>   invalidate.
> 
> Use `[[N]]` refs — several as `[[N]],[[M]]` only when one
> section records multiple commits (a merge non-ff close-out) —
> with the commit URL + 40-hex SHA in the file's `# References`
> (format in AGENTS.md
> [Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references)).
> A section's `##` title matches its commit title, so a rare
> deliberate rewrite of a permanent-branch commit re-syncs via
> `git log --grep "<title>"`.
> 
> The per-push cadence is a project choice, not dogma — a
> **per-close-out** model (recording a cycle's SHAs at its
> close-out) is equally valid. The one invariant: a recorded
> SHA must be permanent.

> **Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
> cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
> happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
> published and explicitly approved that specific push.
> Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
> the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.
> 
> **Default is interactive; an explicit scoped delegation waives
> the gates.** The gates above — per-push approval, the
> commit-description review (show title+body and stop), and the
> hard stop after push/finalize — are the *interactive default*.
> They yield when the user **explicitly** delegates a complete,
> bounded task and authorizes carrying it through ("do all of X
> and push each step, don't check in"). The bot then proceeds
> through that task's commits and pushes without stopping, and
> continues past each push to the next step. Conditions:
> 
> - **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
>   well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
>   completion. "Commit and push" (or "then push") names the
>   destination, not a waiver: it authorizes the push *after*
>   the normal work review and description review, not skipping
>   them. Only wording that explicitly waives the stops ("don't
>   check in", "no need to review", "carry it through
>   unattended") waives them.
> - **Bounded goal** — covers the named task only; does not carry
>   to the next task or a vaguer follow-on.
> - **Destructive ops still pause** — delegation covers the task's
>   ordinary commits and pushes; it does *not* pre-authorize a
>   genuinely irreversible action (force-push over published
>   history, history rewrite, deleting a remote branch). Those can
>   permanently destroy work and aren't a normal cycle step, so the
>   bot flags one before acting. An ordinary delegated cycle never
>   reaches this.
> - **Still transparent** — report each commit/push as it lands
>   (title + outcome) so the user can catch up.
> - **When in doubt, ask** — ambiguous authorization falls back to
>   per-push approval.
>

399,426c470,504
< After a **push** (crossing the remote boundary, by hand or
< via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — whose last stage publishes
< the bot repo too) or a manual **squash-push** on the bot
< repo, stop for the turn: no next step, edit, tool call, or
< text output until the user directs otherwise. **Even when
< the next step seems obvious — wait.**
< 
< - **Scope**: the stop follows the user's directive, not the
<   push. A standing directive covering more work ("finish
<   the remaining ladder commits on your own") makes an
<   intermediate push just a step; the hard stop lands on the
<   turn's *final* push.
< - **Why**: the bot repo is a live journal — everything after
<   the invocation (its own record, closing words) lands in
<   `@` as a trailing tail. Between delegated pushes the tail
<   rides into the next cycle's bot commit; the final push's
<   tail has no next commit, and the bot's own squash-push is
<   itself session data (`@` refills immediately), so only the
<   user, after the turn, can capture it
<   (`vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`).
< - **Silence**: put all closing words *before* the final
<   push. There is no "harmless" closing line after it — a
<   known slip.
< - **Flush**: when the user wants `@` empty (no tail), they
<   run `vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude` after the bot goes
<   quiet — it flushes all bot session information into the
<   published commit. Repeat if new writes land (see
<   [Recovery](#recovery)).
---
> After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
> **finalize** on the bot repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
> .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
> hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
> proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
> user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
> step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
> stop for the whole turn.
> 
> `vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
> then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
> no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
> closing words *before* invoking it. The harness rejects an empty
> turn, so it may force a visible token after the tool returns; if
> so, emit a bare acknowledgment only (e.g. "landed") — never a
> summary, verification, or next-step offer.
> 
> Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
> squashes → pushes):
> 
> - **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
>   harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
>   tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
>   the invoke — into the snapshot.
> - **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
>   flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
>   the bot to do more work.
> - **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
>   after the tool returns. The harness may force a visible token
>   (it rejects an empty turn) — keep it to a bare acknowledgment,
>   never a summary or a next step.
> 
> **Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
> finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
> saying, say it *before* the invoke.

<   run `vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude` after the bot goes
<   quiet — it flushes all bot session information into the
<   published commit. Repeat if new writes land (see
<   [Recovery](#recovery)).
---
> After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
> **finalize** on the bot repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
> .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
> hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
> proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
> user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
> step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
> stop for the whole turn.
> 
> `vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
> then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
> no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
> closing words *before* invoking it. The harness rejects an empty
> turn, so it may force a visible token after the tool returns; if
> so, emit a bare acknowledgment only (e.g. "landed") — never a
> summary, verification, or next-step offer.
> 
> Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
> squashes → pushes):
> 
> - **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
>   harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
>   tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
>   the invoke — into the snapshot.
> - **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
>   flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
>   the bot to do more work.
> - **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
>   after the tool returns. The harness may force a visible token
>   (it rejects an empty turn) — keep it to a bare acknowledgment,
>   never a summary or a next step.
> 
> **Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
> finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
> saying, say it *before* the invoke.

aiTitle: Debug vc-x1 push lock release issue

input.description: See the tail of the diff and template notes dir

content.content:
< multi-line trailer design (multi-user / forking).
< 
< Path semantics — paths start with `/`, the workspace root
< (the work repo); `/.claude` is the bot sub-repo:
< 
< - `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **work repo**.
< - `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the
<   **`.claude` repo**.
< 
< Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the
< end of the commit body, using the chid's **12-character**
< prefix:
< 
< ```
< ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqowktp   # points to a .claude change
< ochid: /wtpmottvxqzl           # points to a work-repo change
< ```
< 
< How many, and which direction:
< 
< - **Code-side commits** each carry one
<   `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
<   change ID.
< - **The `.claude` commit** has one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
<   per code commit in that push. More than one occurs on
<   Merge non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in
<   the cycle).
< 
< Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change
< IDs (first line work repo, second `.claude`).
< 
< ### Resolvability
< 
< A change ID travels with its commit: a **pushed** commit
< resolves to the same chid in every clone — cloning the
< `.claude` repo gave the published `main` tip the same chid
< as an existing clone. The bot thinks jj carries the change
< ID in the git commit object, so it survives
< `jj git clone` / fetch.
< 
< The local-only case is the **working-copy `@`**: jj mints a
< fresh random chid for `@` in each clone, so an unpushed `@`
< is never a stable ochid target. This is why a `.claude`
< ochid names `@-` (the last committed change), not `@`.
< 
< ### .vc-config.toml
< 
< Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
< location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
< without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:
< 
< ```toml
< # In vc-x1 (workspace root):
< [workspace]
< path = "/"
< 
< # In .claude (sub-repo):
< [workspace]
< path = "/.claude"
< ```
< 
552c560
< single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix
---
> single-commit scope — the version
570,572c578,581
< 3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** — build
<    and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
<    commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
---

>    --bins`). **Non-negotiable** — for code, build and clippy
>    alone miss regressions until a later commit runs the full
>    suite, raising bisection cost.
574,576c583,584
<    only (no version suffix); the sub-cycle Close-out
<    collects everything into one final commit with the
<    `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker.
---
>    only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
>    into one final commit.
629c637
< - [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
---
> - [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets) — revset primitives
631,639c639,642
< - [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
<   trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
< - [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
<   scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
<   for squash-recipe experiments.
< - 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-commit ladder usage. The
<   per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
<   after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
<   caught until a later one ran the full suite.
---
> - The per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate exists because a
>   regression introduced in an early ladder commit can go
>   uncaught until a later commit runs the full suite, raising
>   bisection cost.
===
